\title{Tractable Circuit Zoo: Language Definitions}

\maketitle

\begin{definition}[\langref{CNF}]\label{def:lang_89649e36}
\textbf{Conjunctive Normal Form} \\
A standard logical representation where a formula is a conjunction of clauses, where a clause is a disjunction of literals (i.e. the set $\{\bigwedge_{i=1}^m \bigvee_{j=1}^n l_{i,j}\}$, where each $l_{i,j} = x_k$ or $\neg x_k$, for some $x_k \in PS$ \citet{Boole_1847}. Note that \langref{CNF} $\subset$ \langref{NNF}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{cSDD}]\label{def:lang_83e3b023}
\textbf{Compressed Sentential Decision Diagram} \\
The set of compressed SDDs, respecting any vtree: $T$ \langref{cSDD} $= \bigcup_T \langfam{cSDD}{T}$.
\citet{VanDenBroeck_2015,Darwiche_2011}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{cSDD}{T}]\label{def:lang_82fa749e}
\textbf{Compressed Sentential Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
For each fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{cSDD}{T} is the subset of \langfam{SDD}{T} that is \emph{compressed} (no two elements share equivalent subs), together with either normalization or trimming.
Under fixed $T$, compressed+normalized and compressed+trimmed forms are canonical. They are also efficiently inter-convertible, so claims apply to either convention. \citet{Darwiche_2011}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{d-DNNF}]\label{def:lang_6c130090}
\textbf{Deterministic Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
A subset of \langref{DNNF} where the children of every $\vee$ node represent mutually exclusive sub-formulas. \citet{Darwiche_2001a}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{d-SDNNF}]\label{def:lang_ea9b5299}
\textbf{Deterministic Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
The set of \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formulas, respecting any vtree $T$: \langref{d-SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}$.
\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}]\label{def:lang_91f812d0}
\textbf{Deterministic Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
For each fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} is the subset of \langref{d-DNNF} that respects $T$. Equivalently, it is the deterministic subset of \langfam{SDNNF}{T} (see \langref{d-DNNF}). \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{dec-DNNF}]\label{def:lang_981b62f0}
\textbf{Decision Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
A subset of \langref{DNNF} where $\vee$ nodes are entirely replaced by decision nodes, similar to those found in BDDs. \citet{Oztok_2014}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{dec-SDNNF}]\label{def:lang_0f27d539}
\textbf{Structured Decision Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
The language obtained by taking the union of every vtree: \langref{dec-SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}$.
\citet{Oztok_2014}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}]\label{def:lang_4ae03bc8}
\textbf{Structured Decision Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
For each fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} is the subset of \langref{dec-DNNF} that respects $T$. Equivalently, it is the decision fragment of \langfam{SDNNF}{T}. \citet{Oztok_2014}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{DNF}]\label{def:lang_4c204bf3}
\textbf{Disjunctive Normal Form} \\
A standard logical representation where a formula is a disjunction of terms, where a term is a conjunction of literals (i.e. the set $\{\bigvee_{i=1}^m \bigwedge_{j=1}^n l_{i,j}\}$, where each $l_{i,j} = x_k$ or $\neg x_k$, for some $x_k \in PS$) \citet{Boole_1847}. Note that \langref{DNF} $\subset$ \langref{NNF}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{DNNF}]\label{def:lang_3bebcab7}
\textbf{Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
The subset of \langref{NNF} where the children of every $\wedge$ node operate on disjoint sets of variables. \citet{Darwiche_2001a}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{FBDD}]\label{def:lang_684b1ca7}
\textbf{Free Binary Decision Diagram} \\
A Binary Decision Diagram (i.e. circuit of decision nodes) that allows variables to be tested in any order along any given path, provided no variable is tested more than once per path. This property is called the read-once property \citet{Gergov_1994}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{IP}]\label{def:lang_6ae90adc}
\textbf{Prime Implicants} \\
The subset of \langref{DNF} where formulas represent exactly  the disjunction of all minimal entailing terms (prime implicants). \citet{Quine_1952}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{MODS}]\label{def:lang_e02902d0}
\textbf{Models} \\
The set of satisfying truth assignments for a given formula. It may be interpreted as the disjunction of each model, and thus as a \langref{d-SDNNF} formula \citet{Darwiche_2002}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{nFBDD}]\label{def:lang_1df07cc3}
\textbf{Nondeterministic Free Binary Decision Diagram} \\
A generalization of \langref{FBDD} that allows for nondeterministic $\vee$ "guess" nodes in addition to standard variable decisions. \citet{Wegener_2000}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{NNF}]\label{def:lang_5bf00851}
\textbf{Negation Normal Form} \\
A boolean formula constructed strictly with $\wedge$, $\vee$, and literals, where negations are only allowed directly on the variables. It serves as the broad superset for most tractable circuits \citet{Darwiche_2002}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{nOBDD}]\label{def:lang_d24efe0e}
\textbf{Nondeterministic Ordered Binary Decision Diagram} \\
The set of nOBDD formulas following any variable order $<$: \langref{nOBDD} $= \bigcup_{<} \langfam{nOBDD}{<}$.
\citet{Wegener_2000}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{nOBDD}{<}]\label{def:lang_e827cf31}
\textbf{Nondeterministic Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)} \\
For each fixed variable order $<$, \langfam{nOBDD}{<} is the set of \langref{nFBDD} formulas where every root-to-sink path tests variables in an order consistent with $<$. \citet{Wegener_2000}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{OBDD}]\label{def:lang_b9d72a7c}
\textbf{Ordered Binary Decision Diagram} \\
The set of \langfam{OBDD}{<} formulas following any variable order $<$ \langref{OBDD} $= \bigcup_{<} \langfam{OBDD}{<}$. \citet{Bryant_1986,Darwiche_2002}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{OBDD}{<}]\label{def:lang_d69995dd}
\textbf{Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)} \\
For each fixed variable order $<$, \langfam{OBDD}{<} is the set of \langref{FBDD} formulas where every root-to-sink path tests variables in an order consistent with $<$. In an \langref{FBDD}, the order may vary by path; in \langfam{OBDD}{<}, the order is fixed by $<$. \citet{Bryant_1986}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{PI}]\label{def:lang_27fffab2}
\textbf{Prime Implicates} \\
The subset of \langref{CNF} where formulas represent exactly the disjunction of all minimal entailed clauses (prime implicates). \citet{Quine_1952}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{SDD}]\label{def:lang_1afefbe2}
\textbf{Sentential Decision Diagram} \\
The set of \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas respecting any vtree $T$: \langref{SDD} $= \bigcup_T \langfam{SDD}{T}$.
\citet{Darwiche_2011}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{SDD}{T}]\label{def:lang_9c84a267}
\textbf{Sentential Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
For each variable tree $T$, \langfam{SDD}{T} is the subset of \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formulas where each node corresponds to a X-Y (or prime-sub) partition. Specifically, $f \in $ \langfam{SDD}{T} $\Leftrightarrow f(X, Y) = \bigvee_{i=1}^{n}(p_i \land s_i)$, where $p_i$ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive "primes" over X, and $s_i$ are "subs" over Y. \citet{Darwiche_2011}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{SDNNF}]\label{def:lang_b13b0d78}
\textbf{Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form} \\
The set of \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas respecting any vtree $T$: \langref{SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T \langfam{SDNNF}{T}$.
\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{SDNNF}{T}]\label{def:lang_3c803ba1}
\textbf{Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)} \\
For each fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{SDNNF}{T} is the subset of \langref{DNNF} that respects $T$. \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{uFBDD}]\label{def:lang_4e62a038}
\textbf{Unambiguous Free Binary Decision Diagram} \\
A restricted form of \langref{nFBDD} where every satisfying assignment has exactly one valid accepting path. This is equivalent to: for every $\lor$-node of the form $f_1 \lor f_2$, $f_1$ and $f_2$ are mutually exclusive. \citet{Wegener_2000}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langref{uOBDD}]\label{def:lang_c2df8c2b}
\textbf{Unambiguous Ordered Binary Decision Diagram} \\
The set of \langfam{uOBDD}{<} formulas respecting any order $<$: \langref{uOBDD} $= \bigcup_{<} \langfam{uOBDD}{<}$.
\citet{Wegener_2000}.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\langfam{uOBDD}{<}]\label{def:lang_43a33aec}
\textbf{Unambiguous Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)} \\
For each fixed variable order $<$, \langfam{uOBDD}{<} is the subset of \langfam{nOBDD}{<} with the unambiguity property. Equivalently, this is the set of \langref{uFBDD} formulas respecting the order $<$. \citet{Wegener_2000}.
\end{definition}
